student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
patient:
A typical day of eating for me usually starts with a pretty balanced breakfast - oatmeal with fruit and nuts, scrambled eggs, or whole-grain toast with avocado. I try to pack healthy lunches for work when I'm feeling up to it, but since I've been working from home so much lately, I've been relying on leftovers or easy-to-prepare meals like soup or salads.

When I do eat lunch, I usually aim for something light and easy to digest, like a sandwich or a salad with lean protein. Dinner is often the biggest meal of my day, and I try to stick to whole grains, vegetables, and lean proteins - think grilled chicken or fish with roasted veggies, or a hearty bowl of lentil soup.

I have to admit, since getting sick, I've been leaning more heavily on comfort foods like crackers, soups, and toast because they're easy to swallow when my throat is sore. And sometimes, when the coughing fits are really bad, I'll skip meals altogether if I'm feeling too tired or uncomfortable to eat.

student: I'm the doctor handling your case today. Tell me exactly what's going on.
patient: I've been having this crampy lower abdominal pain, mainly on my left side, for about three weeks now. It's gotten worse over time and is really bothering me, plus I've also noticed that when I go to the bathroom, there's blood in my stool.

withheld_facts.content: Nocturnal diarrhea waking him from sleep 2-3 times per night
Tenesmus — sensation of incomplete evacuation after most bowel movements
Unintentional weight loss of about 4 kg (9 lbs) over 3 weeks
Achy pain in both knees and ankles, no swelling or redness
Low-grade fever (37.8-38.0°C), a few times per week
Takes ibuprofen 2-3 times per week for the joint pain, which seems to worsen the abdominal symptoms
Drinks 2-3 beers most weeknights, has increased to 4-5 per night over the past month due to stress from symptoms and work
Older sister diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30

student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
patient: It usually stays in the center of my chest, around where I feel this pressure sensation. Occasionally, during the more severe episodes, I might get a bit of tingling in my left arm, but that's not always the case.

patient: I actually had no GI issues during or after the trip to Mexico for a work conference three months ago, which is good news, I suppose.

student: We've covered a lot. Is there anything we haven't discussed that you think is relevant?
patient: You know, I wish I had thought to mention my driving habits and the stress that comes with it earlier in our conversation. As a truck driver, I'm on the road for long periods, dealing with traffic, bad weather, and tight deadlines, which can be really stressful. It's possible that this stress could be contributing to my episodes of chest tightness, don't you think?

student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
patient: To be honest, my appetite has really taken a hit over the past 5 days. Even thinking about food feels overwhelming to me right now. I usually try to stick to healthy snacks and meals, but lately, I've just been grabbing whatever is easiest or quickest, which isn't exactly ideal for someone who loves cooking like I do. Today, for instance, I ended up having a bowl of cereal for breakfast because it was just too much effort to make something more substantial.

student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?

student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
patient: To be honest, it's been tough for me to stay on top of my meds since I've been driving all over the country for work. I try to remember to take them every day, but sometimes I'll miss a dose or two when I'm on the road and things get crazy. And with the Amlodipine for my high blood pressure, I have to admit that I don't always take it as directed because of my irregular schedule...

student: Chest tightness that comes and goes — that's an important pattern. When you say pressure, is it always in the same spot?
patient: The chest tightness is usually substernal, right behind my breastbone. It feels like a heavy weight pressing down on me, making it hard to breathe when I'm exerting myself or even just walking up stairs.

student: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
patient: When these episodes happen, my body feels like it's under attack - everything gets tight and crampy down there. I feel this intense urge to go to the bathroom right away, and even after I've had a bowel movement, the pain doesn't really go away; it just kind of... lingers, making me feel anxious and uncomfortable for hours afterwards.

student: Your diet directly impacts your health. What do you eat on a daily basis?
patient: Honestly, it's mostly fast food for me when I'm on the road. Burgers, fries, pizza - whatever's quick and easy to grab. At home with my wife, we try to cook healthier meals, but when I'm driving from town to town, that's just not an option. And sometimes, if I'm lucky, I'll stop at a truck stop or diner that has some decent options, but for the most part, it's fast food all day, every day.
